title: The Problem of Evil
challenge: If God is all-powerful, all-knowing, and perfectly good, why does evil exist? The existence of gratuitous suffering — innocent children dying of disease, genocide, natural disasters — seems incompatible with a loving creator. Either God cannot prevent evil (not omnipotent), will not prevent evil (not good), or does not exist. This is the most emotionally and intellectually powerful objection to theism, pressed by philosophers from Epicurus to J.L. Mackie.
response: Christians distinguish between the logical problem of evil (the claim that God and evil are logically incompatible) and the evidential problem (the claim that the amount and distribution of evil makes God's existence improbable). Alvin Plantinga's free will defense decisively answers the logical problem: it is possible that God, even being omnipotent, could not create free beings who always choose good — and a world with free creatures who sometimes choose evil may be more valuable than a world of moral automatons. The evidential problem is more pressing but not unanswerable. God may have morally sufficient reasons for permitting evils that we, with finite knowledge, cannot discern — the 'noseeum' fallacy (if I can't see a reason, there is none) does not follow. More theologically: Christianity does not promise immunity from suffering but gives it meaning. The cross stands as God's entry into suffering — not a theodicy from a distance but solidarity. The resurrection gives grounds for the redemption of suffering in the final accounting.

title: Divine Hiddenness
challenge: If God exists and desires relationship with humanity, why does he not make his existence more evident? Many sincere, open-minded people fail to find God despite genuinely seeking him. J.L. Schellenberg argues that a perfectly loving God would not permit non-resistant non-belief — the existence of honest atheists is evidence against a relational God. God's apparent hiddenness seems inconsistent with the Christian portrayal of a God who actively pursues human beings.
response: The hiddenness argument assumes that if God existed and desired relationship, he would provide overwhelming evidential coercion. But coercive evidence may be incompatible with the kind of freely chosen relationship God values. C.S. Lewis observed that God is 'the Great Iconoclast' — he dismantles idols of our own construction, including our preferred image of him. Theologians like Michael Rea argue that God's modes of presence are varied and personal, not uniform. Some non-belief may not be 'non-resistant' in the deep sense Schellenberg requires — cultural formation, emotional resistance, and pride can create genuine phenomenological non-belief without constituting innocent seeking. The Christian tradition also affirms divine accommodation: God meets persons where they are, at the pace and in the mode appropriate to their formation. The Psalms are replete with laments about divine hiddenness — this is not a modern problem but a thread within faith itself, not a refutation of it.

title: The Exclusivity of Christianity
challenge: How can Christianity claim to be the only path to salvation when billions of people have lived and died without ever hearing the gospel? This seems arbitrary and unjust. Furthermore, religious diversity — the fact that one's religion is largely determined by geography and culture of birth — suggests that religious belief is culturally conditioned rather than tracking objective truth. The claim that salvation comes through faith in Jesus Christ alone (Acts 4:12) seems morally repugnant when applied to sincere followers of other traditions.
response: The exclusivity objection conflates epistemological questions (how do we know which religion is true?) with soteriological ones (who is saved?). Christianity's claim that salvation is in Christ does not require that all who are saved have explicit knowledge of Christ before death — Christian theology has long debated the scope of implicit faith and God's justice toward those without access to the gospel. Romans 2 speaks of conscience as a form of moral knowledge, and Acts 17 suggests God has not left himself without witness. The geographical accident objection cuts both ways: if I would have been a Buddhist had I been born in Thailand, I would also have been a flat-earther had I been born in ancient Egypt — the genetic fallacy does not settle whether a belief is true. Christianity's claim to exclusivity is a claim about objective reality — that Jesus actually rose from the dead — not about cultural superiority. If Jesus did rise, the implications are universal regardless of cultural accident.

title: Miracles and the Scientific Worldview
challenge: Modern science operates on the principle of methodological naturalism — the assumption that natural phenomena have natural causes. Miracles, as violations of natural law, are by definition unscientific and therefore irrational to believe. David Hume argued that no amount of testimony could make a miracle credible, since the improbability of the miracle always outweighs the reliability of the witnesses. A scientifically literate person in the 21st century cannot rationally believe in walking on water, virgin birth, or resurrection.
response: Hume's argument contains a circular premise: it assumes the regularity of nature in order to assign prior probability, but miracles are precisely claims that the regular order has been interrupted. The argument against miracles from science confuses methodological naturalism (a useful working procedure) with metaphysical naturalism (a philosophical claim about the nature of reality). Science describes the regular operation of natural causes — it is not equipped to rule out the occasional action of a cause beyond nature. C.S. Lewis observed that a miracle does not 'break' a natural law any more than dropping a stone into a pond 'breaks' the law of gravitation — it introduces a new element into the equation. The question is whether a God capable of creating the universe is capable of acting within it. If the Christian God exists, miracles are not improbable — they are precisely what a personal creator might be expected to do at crucial moments. The resurrection, as argued elsewhere, is supported by historical evidence that must be evaluated on its merits, not ruled out in advance by philosophical fiat.
